questions.explanation: "My parents" is plural (they), so the auxiliary is "have", and the regular participle of visit is "visited": have visited. "Has" is wrong for a plural subject, and "visit" without -ed is the base form, not a participle.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Rania ___ lots of photos of the castle." (verb: take)
questions.explanation: Rania is one person (she) → "has", and the irregular participle of take is "taken": has taken. "Took" is the simple past (not a participle), "have" is the wrong auxiliary for she, and "take" is the base form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The plane ___ landed; we are collecting our bags now."
questions.explanation: "Just" sits between the auxiliary and the participle, and the plane (it) takes "has": has just landed ✓. "Just has" and "landed just" put the word in the wrong place, and "have" does not agree with "the plane".

questions.prompt: Only ONE sentence is correct. Which one?
questions.explanation: "I" takes "have", "never" goes before the participle, and the participle of eat is "eaten": I have never eaten ✓. "Eat" is the base form, "has" is wrong with I, and "ate" is the simple past, not the participle.

questions.prompt: Complete: "We ___ souvenirs for our family in Tunis." (verb: buy)
questions.explanation: "We" → "have", and the irregular participle of buy is "bought": have bought ✓. "Has" is wrong for we, "buyed" invents a regular ending for an irregular verb, and "buy" is the base form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "'Could you ___ me what time breakfast starts?' asked the tourist."
questions.explanation: To ask for information politely we say "Could you tell me…?" — "tell" takes a person (tell me). "Say" is not used with a person like this ("say me" is wrong), and "talk/speak" do not fit the pattern "___ me what time…".
